APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): Yes — the paper addresses semantic graph parsing (MRS and AMR), a central topic in top NLP/ML venues, and presents a neural encoder-decoder transition-based parser which is directly relevant to the community. It evaluates on standard datasets (DeepBank/ERG and LDC2015E86 AMR) and uses expected metrics (EDM, Smatch) and competitive baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers), matching methodological and evaluation norms. The problem formulation (incremental transition-based parsing with pointer-network alignments) and comparisons are appropriate for the venue’s audience, and the work is positioned within the relevant research threads on semantic parsing and graph representations. | B(5): The paper addresses neural semantic graph parsing for MRS and AMR, a core NLP problem well within top-tier NLP/ML venues. It proposes a neural encoder-decoder transition-based parser with pointer-network alignment and compares against established baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers) using standard datasets (DeepBank, LDC2015E86) and evaluation metrics (EDM, Smatch). The methodology, evaluation standards, and positioning against related work match expectations for a top conference audience interested in semantic representation and parsing.

It evaluates on standard datasets (DeepBank/ERG and LDC2015E86 AMR) and uses expected metrics (EDM, Smatch) and competitive baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers), matching methodological and evaluation norms. The problem formulation (incremental transition-based parsing with pointer-network alignments) and comparisons are appropriate for the venue’s audience, and the work is positioned within the relevant research threads on semantic parsing and graph representations.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses neural semantic graph parsing for MRS and AMR, a core NLP problem well within top-tier NLP/ML venues. It proposes a neural encoder-decoder transition-based parser with pointer-network alignment and compares against established baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers) using standard datasets (DeepBank, LDC2015E86) and evaluation metrics (EDM, Smatch). The methodology, evaluation standards, and positioning against related work match expectations for a top conference audience interested in semantic representation and parsing.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper claims and appears to deliver a new combination of techniques—a transition-based, incrementally decoding neural parser adapted for full-coverage MRS parsing with stack-based embeddings and pointer-network alignment—that is not described in the listed prior work. Framing this as the first full-coverage neural semantic graph parser for MRS and jointly predicting unlexicalized predicates with token alignments clearly differentiates it from existing AMR parsers and grammar-based ERG approaches. The use of an arc-eager variant and hard/soft attention alignment is a non-trivial extension of encoder-decoder and transition-based methods rather than a straightforward reapplication. Overall, the contribution is a notable and well-differentiated advance though not a radical theoretical breakthrough. | B(4): The paper appears to introduce a notable novel combination: a transition-based incremental neural parser that is claimed to be the first full-coverage semantic graph parser for MRS, and it jointly predicts graphs and token alignments using stack-based embeddings and pointer-network style alignment. While it builds on known components (encoder-decoder, arc-eager transitions, pointer networks), adapting them to full-coverage MRS parsing and joint graph/alignment prediction is a non-trivial extension that is clearly differentiated from prior AMR work and grammar-based ERG parsers. The empirical gains and speed improvements further support that the contribution would be informative to experts rather than purely incremental.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper claims and appears to deliver a new combination of techniques—a transition-based, incrementally decoding neural parser adapted for full-coverage MRS parsing with stack-based embeddings and pointer-network alignment—that is not described in the listed prior work. Framing this as the first full-coverage neural semantic graph parser for MRS and jointly predicting unlexicalized predicates with token alignments clearly differentiates it from existing AMR parsers and grammar-based ERG approaches. The use of an arc-eager variant and hard/soft attention alignment is a non-trivial extension of encoder-decoder and transition-based methods rather than a straightforward reapplication. Overall, the contribution is a notable and well-differentiated advance though not a radical theoretical breakthrough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper appears to introduce a notable novel combination: a transition-based incremental neural parser that is claimed to be the first full-coverage semantic graph parser for MRS, and it jointly predicts graphs and token alignments using stack-based embeddings and pointer-network style alignment. While it builds on known components (encoder-decoder, arc-eager transitions, pointer networks), adapting them to full-coverage MRS parsing and joint graph/alignment prediction is a non-trivial extension that is clearly differentiated from prior AMR work and grammar-based ERG parsers. The empirical gains and speed improvements further support that the contribution would be informative to experts rather than purely incremental.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem by presenting the first full-coverage neural semantic graph parser for MRS and demonstrates strong empirical results (86.69% Smatch on MRS) while being an order of magnitude faster than a high-precision grammar-based ERG parser, which should change practical choices for semantic parsing. Its incremental transition-based architecture that jointly predicts graphs and token alignments (using pointer networks and stack-based features) opens clear new directions for efficient, alignment-aware graph parsing. Reported limitations (partial DeepBank coverage, potentially lossy linearization, and weaker concept prediction for AMR) moderate its breadth but do not undermine its likely strong influence on researchers and practitioners working on semantic representations and fast parsing pipelines. | B(4): This paper addresses an important practical problem by delivering the first full-coverage neural semantic graph parser for MRS and achieves a high 86.69% Smatch while being an order-of-magnitude faster than a high-precision grammar-based ERG parser. Its incremental arc-eager transition model that jointly predicts predicates and token alignments (via pointer networks) and its stronger accuracy versus attention-based baselines make it likely to change how researchers approach MRS and transition-based graph parsing. However, reliance on ERG-limited DeepBank coverage, a potentially lossy graph linearization, and remaining weaknesses on AMR concept prediction limit it from being fully transformative across all semantic parsing work.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem by presenting the first full-coverage neural semantic graph parser for MRS and demonstrates strong empirical results (86.69% Smatch on MRS) while being an order of magnitude faster than a high-precision grammar-based ERG parser, which should change practical choices for semantic parsing. Its incremental transition-based architecture that jointly predicts graphs and token alignments (using pointer networks and stack-based features) opens clear new directions for efficient, alignment-aware graph parsing. Reported limitations (partial DeepBank coverage, potentially lossy linearization, and weaker concept prediction for AMR) moderate its breadth but do not undermine its likely strong influence on researchers and practitioners working on semantic representations and fast parsing pipelines.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses an important practical problem by delivering the first full-coverage neural semantic graph parser for MRS and achieves a high 86.69% Smatch while being an order-of-magnitude faster than a high-precision grammar-based ERG parser. Its incremental arc-eager transition model that jointly predicts predicates and token alignments (via pointer networks) and its stronger accuracy versus attention-based baselines make it likely to change how researchers approach MRS and transition-based graph parsing. However, reliance on ERG-limited DeepBank coverage, a potentially lossy graph linearization, and remaining weaknesses on AMR concept prediction limit it from being fully transformative across all semantic parsing work.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Experiments are broadly comprehensive: the model is evaluated on DeepBank and a standard AMR corpus, compared to a wide range of baselines (grammar-based, transition/seq2seq, attention baselines) and reported with EDM/Smatch and speed comparisons. Some ablation-like analyses are present (hard vs. soft attention, arc-eager vs. baselines), but the summary does not indicate exhaustive sensitivity/ablation studies on all architectural choices. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several concrete limitations are discussed (DeepBank coverage gap, lossy linearization, AMR concept prediction weakness, alignment noise). Reproducibility is partially supported (hyperparameters reported) but code availability is not mentioned, leaving some risk to full replication. | B(4): The paper evaluates the parser on both MRS (DeepBank) and AMR (LDC2015E86), compares to strong baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq, grammar-based parsers), and reports relevant metrics (EDM/Smatch) and speed results, which supports the main claims. It includes model variants (hard vs soft attention, arc-eager transition vs attention baselines) and reports hyperparameters, giving reasonable ablation/sensitivity analysis. The authors also acknowledge important limitations (DeepBank coverage gap, lossy linearization heuristics, concept prediction weaknesses and alignment noise), but code is not mentioned which leaves a minor reproducibility gap despite reported hyperparameters.

Related work coverage is described as thorough, and several concrete limitations are discussed (DeepBank coverage gap, lossy linearization, AMR concept prediction weakness, alignment noise). Reproducibility is partially supported (hyperparameters reported) but code availability is not mentioned, leaving some risk to full replication.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates the parser on both MRS (DeepBank) and AMR (LDC2015E86), compares to strong baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq, grammar-based parsers), and reports relevant metrics (EDM/Smatch) and speed results, which supports the main claims. The authors also acknowledge important limitations (DeepBank coverage gap, lossy linearization heuristics, concept prediction weaknesses and alignment noise), but code is not mentioned which leaves a minor reproducibility gap despite reported hyperparameters.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper's main claims are experimentally supported: a transition-based neural parser is evaluated on DeepBank and AMR with appropriate baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq) and standard metrics (EDM/Smatch), and hyperparameters are reported. There are no complex derivations to verify, and the methodology appears sound overall, though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing and notes important dataset limitations (≈15% of corpus unanalyzable) and lossy graph linearization. A minor concern is that the claim comparing MRS Smatch to an AMR upper-bound risks overgeneralization across representations. Overall the results justify the conclusions with these caveats. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by experiments on DeepBank and LDC2015E86 using standard metrics (EDM, Smatch) and sensible baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers, grammar-based ERG). There are no mathematical derivations to inspect, and the described methodology (arc-eager transition system, stack features, pointer-network alignments) is plausible and evaluated thoroughly; hyperparameters are reported and relevant limitations (15% corpus coverage gap, lossy linearization, alignment noise for AMR) are acknowledged. These caveats suggest minor technical concerns about evaluation coverage and representation loss but do not undermine the primary experimental conclusions, so the work is technically sound with only minor issues.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main claims are experimentally supported: a transition-based neural parser is evaluated on DeepBank and AMR with appropriate baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq) and standard metrics (EDM/Smatch), and hyperparameters are reported. There are no complex derivations to verify, and the methodology appears sound overall, though the summary does not mention statistical significance testing and notes important dataset limitations (≈15% of corpus unanalyzable) and lossy graph linearization. A minor concern is that the claim comparing MRS Smatch to an AMR upper-bound risks overgeneralization across representations. Overall the results justify the conclusions with these caveats.

There are no mathematical derivations to inspect, and the described methodology (arc-eager transition system, stack features, pointer-network alignments) is plausible and evaluated thoroughly; hyperparameters are reported and relevant limitations (15% corpus coverage gap, lossy linearization, alignment noise for AMR) are acknowledged. CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and the main contributions are stated early (title and summary clearly present the encoder-decoder transition parser, stack-based features, and pointer-network alignments). Notation and methodology appear consistent and well-defined (arc-eager variant, stack embeddings, hard/soft attention) and experiments report clear metrics (EDM, Smatch, F1) with hyperparameters provided. Figures/tables and examples are implied to be effective given the thorough related-work coverage and explicit discussion of limitations (lossy graph linearization, alignment noise for AMR), so only minor presentation issues remain, hence a score of 4. | B(4): The paper appears well-organized with a clear intro→method→experiments→conclusion flow and states its main contributions early (full-coverage MRS parser, stack-based embeddings, pointer-network alignments, empirical comparisons). Notation and methodology are presented consistently enough for hyperparameters to be reported and for datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics to be clearly described. Figures/tables/examples seem effective insofar as experiments and comparisons are clearly laid out; writing quality is noted as good and related work coverage is thorough, so only minor presentation details appear lacking from the summary.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): The paper compares to a wide set of appropriate baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers, synchronous HRG, a high-precision ERG parser and attention-based baselines) on the same datasets (DeepBank, LDC2015E86) and evaluation metrics (EDM/Smatch). Comparisons appear fair, related work coverage is described as thorough, hyperparameters are reported, and the paper explicitly acknowledges when prior AMR methods (those using extensive external resources) outperform its concept prediction. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (JAMR, CAMR, sequence-to-sequence AMR parsers, Peng & Gildea’s hyperedge grammar, attention-based baselines and a high-precision ERG parser) using the right datasets (DeepBank, LDC2015E86) and metrics (EDM, Smatch). Comparisons appear fair and the paper explicitly acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or better (e.g. lagging behind state-of-the-art AMR parsers that use extensive external resources) and analyzes causes of differences (noisy alignments hurting arc-eager, concept prediction weaknesses). Coverage of related work is described as thorough, so only minor omissions (e.g. deeper evaluation against the strongest resource-heavy AMR systems) prevent a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares to a wide set of appropriate baselines (JAMR, CAMR, seq2seq AMR parsers, synchronous HRG, a high-precision ERG parser and attention-based baselines) on the same datasets (DeepBank, LDC2015E86) and evaluation metrics (EDM/Smatch). Comparisons appear fair, related work coverage is described as thorough, hyperparameters are reported, and the paper explicitly acknowledges when prior AMR methods (those using extensive external resources) outperform its concept prediction.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and relevant baselines (JAMR, CAMR, sequence-to-sequence AMR parsers, Peng & Gildea’s hyperedge grammar, attention-based baselines and a high-precision ERG parser) using the right datasets (DeepBank, LDC2015E86) and metrics (EDM, Smatch). Comparisons appear fair and the paper explicitly acknowledges when prior methods perform comparably or better (e.g. lagging behind state-of-the-art AMR parsers that use extensive external resources) and analyzes causes of differences (noisy alignments hurting arc-eager, concept prediction weaknesses). Coverage of related work is described as thorough, so only minor omissions (e.g. deeper evaluation against the strongest resource-heavy AMR systems) prevent a top score.

main_claims: We propose a neural encoder-decoder transition-based parser that is the first full-coverage semantic graph parser for Minimal Recursion Semantics (MRS).
The model architecture uses stack-based embedding features and predicts graphs jointly with unlexicalized predicates and their token alignments (using pointer networks/hard attention).
The parser is more accurate than attention-based baselines on MRS and on an AMR benchmark, and GPU batch processing makes it an order of magnitude faster than a high-precision grammar-based parser.
The MRS parser achieves an 86.69% Smatch score, which the paper states is higher than the upper-bound on AMR parsing, suggesting MRS as an attractive semantic representation.
_summary.methodology: A neural encoder-decoder transition-based parser that incrementally predicts semantic graphs and their alignments using a variant of the arc-eager transition system, stack-based embedding features, and pointer-network-based alignment (with hard and soft attention variants).
baselines: Sequence-to-sequence AMR parsers (Barzdins and Gosko, 2016; Peng et al., 2017)
Synchronous Hyperedge Replacement Grammar (Peng and Gildea, 2016)
limitations: DeepBank parses are provided only for sentences the ERG can analyze, so approximately 15% of the original corpus cannot be evaluated.
The graph linearization used can be potentially lossy: reentrancies are represented with dummy nodes and recoveries are heuristic.
Concept prediction remains a weakness for AMR parsing and the model lags behind state-of-the-art AMR parsers that use extensive external resources.
Arc-eager model performance on AMR is hurt by noise in automatically-obtained alignments.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semantic graph parsing (MRS and AMR)
